stem: The duty factor of a pulsed ultrasound system is defined as:
options: Pulse duration divided by pulse repetition period
Power output divided by beam area
rationale: Duty factor = pulse duration / PRP (or PD × PRF). It represents the fraction of time the transducer is actively transmitting. Typical diagnostic duty factor is 0.1-1%.

stem: Increasing the frequency of an ultrasound transducer will:
options: Improve axial resolution but decrease penetration depth
Increase penetration depth and worsen resolution
Have no effect on image quality
Only change the color Doppler sensitivity
rationale: Higher frequency = shorter wavelength = better axial resolution, but higher attenuation in tissue reduces penetration depth. This is the fundamental frequency trade-off.

stem: The propagation speed of ultrasound in soft tissue is approximately:
rationale: The average propagation speed in soft tissue is 1,540 m/s. This value is assumed by the ultrasound system for distance calculations. Variations cause artifacts.

stem: Wavelength is calculated by:
rationale: Wavelength (λ) = speed / frequency. In soft tissue at 5 MHz: λ = 1540/5,000,000 = 0.308 mm. Shorter wavelength provides better resolution.

stem: The intensity of an ultrasound beam is measured in:
rationale: Intensity = Power / Area, expressed in W/cm² or mW/cm². It describes the concentration of energy in the beam. Relevant for bioeffects and safety.

stem: Half-value layer thickness refers to:
options: The tissue thickness that reduces ultrasound intensity by half (3 dB)
The distance at which the beam width doubles
The depth of the focal zone
The length of the near field
rationale: Half-value layer (HVL) is the tissue thickness that attenuates the beam by 50% (3 dB). It depends on tissue type and frequency. Used for estimating penetration.

stem: Specular reflection occurs when:
options: The reflecting surface is large and smooth relative to the wavelength
The reflector is much smaller than the wavelength
The beam passes through homogeneous tissue
The transducer is in continuous wave mode
rationale: Specular reflectors are large, smooth surfaces (e.g., diaphragm, vessel walls). Reflection follows the law of reflection (angle of incidence = angle of reflection).

stem: Rayleigh scattering occurs when the reflector is:
options: Much smaller than the wavelength, causing omnidirectional scatter
Equal in size to the wavelength
rationale: Rayleigh scattering occurs when structures are much smaller than the wavelength (e.g., red blood cells). Scatter is omnidirectional and proportional to the fourth power of frequency.

stem: The mechanical index (MI) is a safety parameter that indicates:
rationale: MI = peak rarefactional pressure / √frequency. It estimates the likelihood of cavitation (mechanical tissue disruption). FDA limit is MI ≤ 1.9 for diagnostic imaging.

stem: The thermal index (TI) represents:
options: The estimated temperature rise in degrees Celsius
The number of thermal cycles per second
rationale: TI estimates the potential temperature rise in tissue. TI of 1.0 suggests tissue may warm by approximately 1°C. Three subtypes: TIS (soft tissue), TIB (bone), TIC (cranial).

stem: Harmonic imaging improves image quality by:
options: Using the second harmonic frequency generated in tissue, reducing artifacts and clutter
rationale: Tissue harmonic imaging uses frequencies generated by nonlinear propagation in tissue (2× fundamental). Harmonics build gradually, bypassing near-field clutter and artifacts.

stem: Time gain compensation (TGC) is used to:
options: Compensate for attenuation by amplifying echoes from deeper structures
rationale: TGC applies variable gain at different depths to produce uniform brightness despite depth-dependent attenuation. Deeper echoes receive more amplification.

stem: Dynamic range in ultrasound refers to:
options: The range of echo amplitudes from weakest to strongest that the system can display
The distance from near field to far field
The number of frames per second
rationale: Dynamic range is measured in decibels. A wider dynamic range displays more shades of gray (softer image). Narrower dynamic range creates higher contrast images.

stem: Compound imaging works by:
options: Acquiring multiple frames from different steering angles and averaging them
Sending continuous wave and pulsed wave together
rationale: Spatial compound imaging steers the beam at multiple angles and averages the resulting images. This reduces speckle noise, improves boundary visualization, and fills in shadowing.

stem: Persistence (frame averaging) on ultrasound:
options: Averages consecutive frames to reduce noise, but decreases temporal resolution
rationale: Persistence smooths the image by averaging multiple frames, reducing random noise (speckle). Trade-off: moving structures may appear blurred due to temporal averaging.

stem: A comet-tail artifact is a type of:
options: Reverberation artifact from small highly reflective interfaces
rationale: Comet-tail artifacts are short-path reverberations between closely spaced reflective interfaces (e.g., cholesterol crystals, metallic objects). They appear as tapering bright echoes.

stem: Ring-down artifact differs from comet-tail in that it:
options: Is caused by resonant vibration of a fluid-gas mixture producing a continuous line
Is caused by two closely spaced reflectors
Never extends beyond 1 cm from the source
rationale: Ring-down artifact arises from resonant vibrations of trapped fluid between bubbles, producing a continuous stream of echoes. Comet-tail artifacts have discrete decreasing bands.

stem: Side-lobe artifacts produce:
options: Low-level echoes within anechoic structures from off-axis energy
rationale: Side lobes are secondary low-energy beams radiating at angles from the main beam. Echoes returning from side lobes are displayed as if from the main beam, creating false echoes in cysts.

stem: Speed of sound artifact occurs when:
options: The actual propagation speed in tissue differs from the assumed 1,540 m/s
rationale: If actual speed differs from 1,540 m/s, structures are misregistered. Higher speed (e.g., bone) places the echo too close; lower speed (e.g., fat) places it too far.

stem: Twinkling artifact on color Doppler is useful for identifying:
options: Calculi and calcifications that produce a rapidly alternating color signal
rationale: Twinkling artifact produces a mosaic of alternating color signals behind rough, highly reflective surfaces like kidney stones and calcifications, aiding detection.

stem: The normal echogenicity relationship from brightest to least bright is:
options: Renal sinus > pancreas > liver > renal cortex > medullary pyramids
rationale: Renal sinus fat is most echogenic. Pancreas is usually isoechoic or slightly more echogenic than liver. Liver is slightly brighter than renal cortex. Medullary pyramids are hypoechoic.

stem: Budd-Chiari syndrome is caused by:
options: Hepatic vein thrombosis leading to hepatic congestion
rationale: Budd-Chiari syndrome results from hepatic vein outflow obstruction (thrombosis). Sonography shows absent hepatic vein flow, caudate lobe hypertrophy, and hepatomegaly.

stem: The caudate lobe is unique because it:
options: Drains directly into the IVC via independent hepatic veins
Is always the largest hepatic lobe
Is located in the left abdomen
rationale: The caudate lobe has separate venous drainage directly into the IVC. In Budd-Chiari syndrome, it hypertrophies because its drainage is preserved.

stem: Portal hypertension findings on ultrasound include:
options: Splenomegaly, portal vein dilation >13mm, reversed portal flow, and ascites
Contracted portal vein with high-velocity flow
rationale: Portal hypertension: portal vein >13mm, hepatofugal (reversed) flow, splenomegaly, recanalized paraumbilical vein, varices, and ascites.

stem: Hepatic steatosis is graded sonographically by comparing liver to:
options: Right kidney cortex echogenicity and assessing deep structure visualization
rationale: Fatty liver grading: Grade 1 = increased echogenicity vs kidney; Grade 2 = obscured portal vein walls; Grade 3 = obscured diaphragm and deep structures.

stem: The main lobar fissure of the liver extends from:
options: Gallbladder fossa to the right portal vein or IVC
Left portal vein to the hepatic artery
Caudate lobe to the left lobe
Right hepatic vein to the diaphragm
rationale: The main lobar fissure separates the right and left hepatic lobes. It extends from the gallbladder fossa anteroinferiorly to the right portal vein or IVC posterosuperiorly.

stem: The ligamentum teres is a remnant of the:
rationale: The ligamentum teres (round ligament) is the obliterated umbilical vein. It appears as an echogenic focus in the left intersegmental fissure of the liver.

stem: Courvoisier sign refers to:
options: Painless jaundice with a palpable distended gallbladder, suggesting malignant biliary obstruction
Thickened gallbladder wall with Murphy sign
rationale: Courvoisier law: a palpable, nontender, distended gallbladder with jaundice suggests malignant obstruction (pancreatic head mass), not gallstones.

stem: Porcelain gallbladder refers to:
options: Calcification of the gallbladder wall, appearing as a curvilinear echogenic structure with shadowing
A gallbladder filled with milk of calcium
rationale: Porcelain gallbladder shows mural calcification producing a curvilinear echogenic wall with posterior shadowing. It carries an increased risk of gallbladder carcinoma.

stem: Gallbladder sludge represents:
options: Concentrated bile with calcium bilirubinate and cholesterol crystals that layers dependently
rationale: Sludge is viscous material that layers in the dependent portion of the GB, moves slowly with position changes, and does not shadow. It indicates bile stasis.

stem: Adenomyomatosis of the gallbladder shows:
options: Focal or diffuse wall thickening with intramural diverticula (Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses) and comet-tail artifacts
rationale: Adenomyomatosis features wall thickening with Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses (intramural diverticula) that produce comet-tail artifacts from cholesterol crystals.

stem: The CBD is measured at the:
options: Anterior to the right portal vein where it crosses
rationale: The CBD is standardly measured where it crosses anterior to the right portal vein. This provides a consistent, reproducible measurement location.

stem: Mirizzi syndrome is caused by:
options: A gallstone impacted in the cystic duct compressing the common hepatic duct
rationale: Mirizzi syndrome: a stone in the cystic duct or GB neck externally compresses the common hepatic duct, causing proximal biliary dilation and jaundice.

stem: Normal adult kidney dimensions are approximately:
rationale: Normal adult kidney length is 9-12 cm (average ~11 cm). A discrepancy of >2 cm between kidneys may indicate pathology. Size decreases in chronic renal disease.

stem: Angiomyolipoma of the kidney appears as:
options: Well-defined small hyperechoic mass without shadowing
rationale: Angiomyolipomas are benign tumors containing fat, smooth muscle, and vessels. The fat content makes them characteristically hyperechoic. May be associated with tuberous sclerosis.

stem: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) sonographically shows:
options: Bilaterally enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts of varying sizes
Unilateral small kidney with single cyst
Normal-sized kidneys with one cortical cyst
rationale: ADPKD: bilaterally enlarged kidneys with numerous cysts of varying sizes replacing normal parenchyma. Liver cysts are common. Progressive renal failure occurs.

stem: The junctional parenchymal defect (junctional line) in the kidney is:
options: A normal echogenic line at the junction of upper and lower pole renal tissue, not pathology
An early sign of renal cell carcinoma
An indication of renal artery stenosis
rationale: The junctional parenchymal defect is a normal variant representing the fusion line of embryonic renunculi. It appears as an echogenic line and should not be mistaken for a scar or mass.

stem: Column of Bertin is:
options: Hypertrophied cortical tissue extending into the renal sinus, a normal variant
rationale: A prominent column of Bertin is a normal variant of hypertrophied cortical tissue that extends between medullary pyramids into the renal sinus. It is isoechoic to cortex.

stem: Medullary nephrocalcinosis appears as:
options: Echogenic medullary pyramids with or without acoustic shadowing
rationale: Medullary nephrocalcinosis shows increased echogenicity of the renal pyramids due to calcium deposition. Causes include hyperparathyroidism, medullary sponge kidney, and RTA.

stem: A duplicated collecting system is identified by:
options: Two separate renal pelvic echogenic complexes within one kidney
Absence of the renal sinus echo
rationale: Duplex kidney shows two echogenic sinus complexes separated by a band of cortical tissue (bridge). The upper pole ureter may insert ectopically, and the lower pole is prone to reflux.

stem: The normal aortic diameter at the level of the renal arteries should not exceed:
rationale: Normal aortic diameter is approximately 2 cm, tapering distally. An anteroposterior diameter ≥3 cm is considered aneurysmal. Screening is recommended for males >65 who have smoked.

stem: An abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is defined as aortic diameter greater than:
options: 3 cm or 50% larger than the proximal normal segment
rationale: AAA is defined as ≥3 cm or >50% dilation compared to normal proximal aorta. Ultrasound is the screening modality of choice. Risk of rupture increases with size.

stem: The IVC normally shows respiratory variation. During inspiration the IVC:
options: Decreases in diameter as negative intrathoracic pressure increases venous return
rationale: During inspiration, negative intrathoracic pressure pulls blood into the thorax, decreasing IVC diameter. A non-collapsing IVC may indicate elevated right atrial pressure or volume overload.

stem: The spleen is best imaged with the patient in:
options: Right lateral decubitus position using a left intercostal approach
Supine position with a subcostal approach
rationale: Right lateral decubitus positioning moves the spleen inferiorly and allows use of the left kidney as an acoustic window through the intercostal spaces.

stem: Accessory spleens (splenunculi) appear as:
options: Small round homogeneous structures isoechoic to the spleen near the splenic hilum
rationale: Splenunculi are small (1-3 cm), round, homogeneous, and have the same echogenicity and vascularity as the parent spleen. Found in ~10% of the population near the splenic hilum.

stem: The normal pancreatic duct diameter in the body of the pancreas should not exceed:
rationale: Normal pancreatic duct (Wirsung) measures ≤2 mm in the body. Dilation suggests obstruction (stone, mass, chronic pancreatitis). Mild dilation may occur in elderly patients.

stem: Chronic pancreatitis findings on ultrasound include:
options: Pancreatic calcifications, dilated irregular pancreatic duct, and atrophic gland
rationale: Chronic pancreatitis: echogenic calcifications (often with shadowing), irregular ductal dilation, parenchymal atrophy, and heterogeneous echotexture from fibrosis.

stem: A pancreatic head mass causing double-duct sign refers to:
options: Simultaneous dilation of both the CBD and pancreatic duct
rationale: The double-duct sign (dilated CBD and pancreatic duct) is highly suspicious for pancreatic head carcinoma obstructing both ducts at the ampulla.

stem: The most common benign cystic neoplasm of the pancreas in women is:
rationale: Serous cystadenomas are benign, composed of numerous tiny cysts with a honeycomb or sponge-like appearance and possible central calcification (sunburst pattern).

stem: The SMA and aorta angle should be greater than _____ to exclude SMA syndrome:
rationale: SMA syndrome occurs when the angle between the SMA and aorta is narrowed (<25°), compressing the duodenum. Normal angle is 38-65°. Can cause duodenal obstruction.

stem: In first trimester, an embryo with CRL ≥7mm should have a visible:
rationale: When CRL reaches ≥7mm on TVUS, cardiac activity should be seen. Absence of heartbeat at this size is consistent with embryonic demise.

stem: Nuchal translucency (NT) measurement is performed at:
rationale: NT is measured between 11w0d-13w6d (CRL 45-84mm). Increased NT (≥3mm) is associated with chromosomal abnormalities, cardiac defects, and other anomalies.

stem: A complete molar pregnancy typically shows:
options: Snowstorm or grape-like vesicular pattern filling the uterus without a fetus
Single gestational sac with yolk sac
rationale: Complete mole: swollen villi fill the uterus creating a 'snowstorm' or 'cluster of grapes' appearance. No fetus present. Markedly elevated beta-hCG. Risk of GTD.

stem: Subchorionic hemorrhage on ultrasound appears as:
options: Crescent-shaped hypoechoic or complex collection between the chorion and uterine wall
rationale: Subchorionic hemorrhage presents as a crescent-shaped collection adjacent to the gestational sac. Echogenicity varies with age of hemorrhage. Most resolve spontaneously.

stem: The amnion and chorion normally fuse by:
rationale: The amnion and chorion are separate membranes that fuse by 14-16 weeks. Persistent separation beyond 16 weeks may indicate chorioamniotic separation.

stem: Biparietal diameter (BPD) is measured from:
options: Outer edge of near skull table to inner edge of far skull table at the level of the thalami
At the level of the cerebellum
rationale: BPD is measured at the level of the thalami and cavum septum pellucidum, from outer edge of near calvarial wall to inner edge of far wall (leading edge to leading edge).

stem: Femur length that measures significantly shorter than expected for gestational age may indicate:
rationale: Short femur may indicate skeletal dysplasia (achondroplasia, osteogenesis imperfecta), trisomy 21, trisomy 18, or IUGR. Clinical correlation with other findings is essential.

stem: The estimated fetal weight (EFW) is calculated using:
options: Head circumference, abdominal circumference, and femur length (Hadlock formula)
rationale: Hadlock formula uses HC, AC, and FL to estimate fetal weight. AC is the single most important parameter for weight estimation. Accuracy is ±10-15%.

stem: The placental grade III changes described by Grannum include:
options: Calcifications indenting the basal plate with echo-free areas between cotyledons
rationale: Grade III placenta shows extensive calcifications along the basal plate with indentations, creating distinct cotyledon compartments. May be seen in post-dates pregnancies.

stem: Cervical length less than 25mm at 16-24 weeks is associated with:
rationale: Short cervix (<25mm by TVUS) between 16-24 weeks is a predictor of preterm birth. Funneling (dilation of internal os) increases the risk further.

stem: Placental abruption may appear on ultrasound as:
options: Retroplacental hypoechoic or complex collection with possible placental thickening
rationale: Abruption: retroplacental or subchorionic hematoma. Appearance varies with age. Acute blood may be isoechoic to placenta and difficult to detect. Clinical diagnosis often takes precedence.

stem: Vasa previa is diagnosed when:
options: Fetal vessels cross the internal cervical os, not protected by placental tissue or cord
Umbilical cord prolapses through the cervix
rationale: Vasa previa: fetal vessels traverse the membranes over the internal os. Diagnosed by color Doppler on TVUS. Risk of fatal hemorrhage at membrane rupture.

stem: The middle cerebral artery (MCA) peak systolic velocity is used to assess:
rationale: MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM suggests significant fetal anemia (Rh isoimmunization, parvovirus). It has largely replaced invasive amniocentesis for fetal anemia assessment.

stem: An umbilical artery S/D ratio greater than 3.0 after 30 weeks suggests:
options: Increased placental resistance and possible IUGR
rationale: Elevated umbilical artery S/D ratio (or RI/PI) indicates increased placental vascular resistance. Associated with IUGR, preeclampsia, and adverse fetal outcomes.

stem: The normal umbilical cord contains:
rationale: Normal cord has two umbilical arteries (carrying deoxygenated blood to placenta) and one umbilical vein (carrying oxygenated blood to fetus). Single umbilical artery is associated with anomalies.

stem: ICA stenosis of 50-69% is characterized by PSV of:
rationale: 50-69% ICA stenosis: PSV 125-230 cm/s, EDV 40-100 cm/s, ICA/CCA ratio 2-4. These SRU consensus criteria guide clinical management decisions.

stem: Vertebral artery flow reversal suggests:
options: Subclavian steal syndrome due to proximal subclavian artery stenosis or occlusion
rationale: Subclavian steal: proximal subclavian stenosis/occlusion causes retrograde vertebral artery flow to supply the arm. Patients may have arm claudication and vertebrobasilar symptoms.

stem: ICA occlusion versus near-occlusion can be differentiated by:
options: Absence of any detectable flow in true occlusion versus a thin trickle of flow in near-occlusion
rationale: ICA occlusion shows no detectable flow. Near-occlusion (string sign) shows a tiny residual lumen with low-velocity flow. Power Doppler and contrast may help distinguish them.

stem: The ankle-brachial index (ABI) is calculated by:
options: Highest ankle systolic pressure divided by highest brachial systolic pressure
Ankle diastolic divided by brachial diastolic
Brachial pressure divided by ankle pressure
rationale: ABI = highest ankle systolic pressure (DP or PT) / highest brachial systolic pressure. Normal ≥1.0. ABI <0.9 indicates PAD. ABI >1.4 suggests noncompressible calcified arteries.

stem: Monophasic arterial waveform in the lower extremity suggests:
options: Significant proximal arterial stenosis or occlusion
rationale: Normal peripheral arterial waveform is triphasic. Monophasic (continuous forward flow without reversal) indicates significant proximal obstruction with loss of peripheral resistance.

stem: Pseudoaneurysm on ultrasound shows:
options: A contained rupture with a neck connecting to the artery and to-and-fro (yin-yang) flow pattern
True dilation of all three arterial wall layers
rationale: Pseudoaneurysm: perivascular collection with a communicating neck to the artery. Color Doppler shows yin-yang (swirling) flow within the sac and to-and-fro flow in the neck.

stem: Venous reflux on duplex ultrasound is confirmed when retrograde flow exceeds:
options: 0.5 seconds after distal augmentation or Valsalva
rationale: Venous reflux (insufficiency) is defined as retrograde flow lasting >0.5 seconds (>1.0 second for popliteal vein per some criteria) after augmentation release or Valsalva.

stem: May-Thurner syndrome involves:
options: Compression of the left common iliac vein by the right common iliac artery
Compression of the IVC by the liver
Subclavian vein compression by the first rib
rationale: May-Thurner (Cockett syndrome): the right common iliac artery compresses the left common iliac vein against the lumbar spine, predisposing to left-sided DVT.

stem: Upper extremity DVT risk factors include:
options: Central venous catheters, pacemaker leads, and thoracic outlet syndrome
rationale: Upper extremity DVT causes: central lines, ports, pacemakers, effort thrombosis (Paget-Schroetter), malignancy, and thoracic outlet syndrome. Non-compressibility is the diagnostic criterion.

stem: Paget-Schroetter syndrome is:
options: Effort-related thrombosis of the axillary-subclavian vein in young athletes
Deep vein thrombosis of the lower extremity
rationale: Paget-Schroetter (effort thrombosis): spontaneous upper extremity DVT in the axillary/subclavian vein from repetitive overhead activity or thoracic outlet compression.

stem: The renal artery resistive index (RI) above which value suggests renal pathology?
rationale: RI >0.70 in native kidneys suggests increased renal vascular resistance from medical renal disease, obstruction, or rejection in transplants. Normal RI is 0.50-0.70.

stem: Renal artery stenosis is suggested when the renal-to-aortic velocity ratio (RAR) exceeds:
rationale: RAR >3.5 (renal artery PSV / aortic PSV) or renal artery PSV >180-200 cm/s suggests ≥60% renal artery stenosis. Tardus-parvus waveforms may be seen downstream.

stem: Tardus-parvus waveform in the intrarenal arteries indicates:
options: Proximal renal artery stenosis causing delayed systolic upstroke
rationale: Tardus (delayed) parvus (diminished) waveform shows prolonged acceleration time (>70 ms) and low acceleration index, indicating significant proximal stenosis dampening the waveform.

stem: Graves disease on thyroid ultrasound shows:
options: Diffusely enlarged heterogeneous gland with markedly increased vascularity (thyroid inferno)
Small atrophic gland with no flow
rationale: Graves disease: diffuse enlargement, heterogeneous hypoechoic parenchyma, and dramatically increased color Doppler flow ('thyroid inferno' pattern) from hypervascular state.

stem: The ACR TI-RADS category TR5 (highly suspicious) requires FNA for nodules:
rationale: TR5 (≥7 points): FNA recommended at ≥10mm, follow-up at ≥5mm. Higher TI-RADS scores indicate greater malignancy risk and lower size thresholds for intervention.

stem: A parathyroid adenoma typically appears as:
options: Small hypoechoic oval mass posterior to the thyroid gland with a feeding vessel
Large hyperechoic mass anterior to the thyroid
Cystic mass within the thyroid gland
Calcified mass with shadowing lateral to the thyroid
rationale: Parathyroid adenomas are usually small (1-3 cm), homogeneously hypoechoic, oval, and located posterior to the thyroid. A characteristic polar feeding vessel may be seen.

stem: An epididymal cyst appears as:
options: Small anechoic round structure in the epididymal head with posterior enhancement
Large complex mass replacing the testis
rationale: Epididymal cysts are common benign findings. They appear as small, well-defined, anechoic structures, usually in the epididymal head, with posterior acoustic enhancement.

stem: A spermatocele differs from an epididymal cyst because:
options: It may contain low-level internal echoes from spermatozoa and cellular debris
It is always located in the testis
rationale: Spermatoceles are sperm-containing cysts usually in the epididymal head. They may show low-level internal echoes unlike simple epididymal cysts which are purely anechoic.

stem: Testicular microlithiasis is characterized by:
options: Multiple tiny echogenic foci (≥5 per field) without shadowing within the testicular parenchyma
rationale: Testicular microlithiasis: multiple nonshadowing bright echogenic foci. May be associated with increased risk of germ cell tumors. Follow-up with self-examination is recommended.

stem: Acute epididymitis on ultrasound shows:
options: Enlarged hypoechoic epididymis with increased blood flow on color Doppler
rationale: Acute epididymitis: enlarged, swollen, hypoechoic epididymis with hyperemia on color Doppler. May progress to epididymo-orchitis involving the testis.

stem: A hydrocele appears on ultrasound as:
options: Anechoic fluid surrounding the testis within the tunica vaginalis
rationale: Hydrocele: anechoic fluid collection between the visceral and parietal layers of the tunica vaginalis. May be reactive (infection, trauma, tumor) or idiopathic.

stem: Breast BI-RADS 4 classification indicates:
rationale: BI-RADS 4 = suspicious abnormality; biopsy recommended. Divided into 4a (low), 4b (moderate), 4c (high suspicion). Malignancy likelihood ranges from 2-95%.

stem: A fibroadenoma on breast ultrasound typically appears as:
options: Well-circumscribed, oval, homogeneously hypoechoic solid mass wider than tall
rationale: Fibroadenomas are the most common benign breast masses in young women. Characteristics: oval, parallel orientation (wider than tall), smooth margins, homogeneous, may have gentle lobulations.

stem: Features of a breast mass suspicious for malignancy include:
options: Irregular shape, angular margins, taller-than-wide orientation, posterior acoustic shadowing
Round shape, smooth margins, wider than tall, posterior enhancement
rationale: Suspicious features: irregular shape, non-parallel (taller than wide), angular/spiculated margins, shadowing, calcifications, and architectural distortion.

stem: A complicated breast cyst differs from a simple cyst by:
options: Containing homogeneous low-level internal echoes but having no solid component
Having thick walls and solid nodules
rationale: Complicated cysts have homogeneous low-level echoes (debris, proteinaceous fluid) but lack solid components or vascularity. BI-RADS 3 (probably benign) with short-term follow-up.

stem: FAST (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) examines which spaces?
options: Perihepatic (Morison's pouch), perisplenic, pelvis, and pericardium
rationale: FAST evaluates four areas for free fluid: hepatorenal space (Morison's pouch), splenorenal space, pelvis (pouch of Douglas/rectovesical), and pericardium.

stem: Morison's pouch is the space between:
rationale: Morison's pouch (hepatorenal recess) is the most dependent space in the supine patient's peritoneal cavity. Free fluid collects here first in trauma.

stem: E-FAST adds evaluation of which additional area compared to standard FAST?
rationale: Extended FAST adds bilateral anterior thoracic views to detect pneumothorax (absent lung sliding and barcode/stratosphere sign on M-mode) in addition to standard FAST views.

stem: A seashore sign on M-mode of the lung indicates:
options: Normal lung sliding, ruling out pneumothorax at that location
rationale: Seashore sign (sandy beach): the pleura and lung tissue create a granular pattern below the pleural line on M-mode, indicating normal lung sliding. Barcode/stratosphere sign = pneumothorax.

stem: The sonographer's primary obligation to the patient includes:
options: Maintaining patient dignity, safety, comfort, and confidentiality throughout the exam
rationale: Sonographers are responsible for patient care during the exam: maintaining dignity, ensuring comfort, protecting privacy, and communicating findings to the interpreting physician—not diagnosing.

stem: When scanning a patient with a known latex allergy, the sonographer should:
options: Use non-latex probe covers and gloves
Use standard latex gloves with extra gel
Apply probe directly without any cover
rationale: Patients with latex allergies require non-latex alternatives for probe covers and gloves (e.g., nitrile, polyurethane). Contact with latex can trigger anaphylaxis in sensitized patients.

stem: Proper cleaning of endocavitary transducers between patients requires:
options: Low-level disinfection at minimum, with high-level disinfection recommended per guidelines
Only wiping with a dry cloth
rationale: Endocavitary probes contact mucous membranes and require high-level disinfection (HLD) between patients per ACR/AIUM guidelines. A barrier/cover should also be used during the exam.

stem: Spatial peak temporal average intensity (ISPTA) is considered the most relevant intensity parameter for:
options: Assessing thermal bioeffects during diagnostic imaging
rationale: ISPTA represents the highest intensity at any point in the beam averaged over time. It is the most relevant parameter for thermal bioeffects because tissue heating depends on time-averaged energy.

stem: The near field (Fresnel zone) length depends on:
options: Transducer diameter and frequency (larger aperture and higher frequency = longer near field)
rationale: Near field length = D²f / (4c) where D = aperture diameter, f = frequency, c = speed of sound. Larger transducer and higher frequency produce a longer near field.

stem: In the far field (Fraunhofer zone), the beam:
options: Diverges, resulting in decreased lateral resolution
rationale: Beyond the focal point (far field), the beam diverges. Beam width increases, worsening lateral resolution. Multiple focal zones can extend the region of best resolution.
